narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because a model sees a 55% chance you'll struggle with the bar exam feels deeply unfair, especially when you've poured so much into getting here. Picture the model looking at your story like a scout reviewing a promising athlete's tape: what stands out most is your Year 3 decile of 2.0, the biggest signal pulling toward failure, suggesting that even after building momentum, your grades placed you near the bottom of your class when it mattered most. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 30.0 raised flags, as it's on the lower side of what's typically needed to power through the bar's demands. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 added to that early pattern, showing consistency in the middle-to-lower pack rather than the climb admissions teams hope for. Coming from a middle-income family nudged things further in a riskier direction, as the model links that bracket with added hurdles in high-stakes legal prep. Your Asian background and undergraduate GPA of 2.8 layered on more caution, painting a picture of someone who's resilient but facing steeper odds statistically. Even as a full-time student and a woman pursuing this path—factors that often fuel success—your gender and commitment didn't outweigh the stack of challenges the model weighed heaviest. It's not that you're incapable; it's that this specific blend, led by those deciles and LSAT, mirrored profiles of others who hit roadblocks on the bar. The good news? Law school isn't the only door—your target law passion shines through, and targeted prep could flip this script elsewhere. You've got the drive; now it's about finding the right arena to prove the model wrong. Hang in there.
